socket_gateway.role: Socket Gateway aggregator proxy. Held thousands of pre-existing user approvals. The vulnerable route's performAction trusted a caller-supplied fromUser parameter.

attacker.role: Attacker EOA. Repeatedly invoked the vulnerable route with fromUser=victimAddress for each previously-approved user.

single_chain_note.reason: Socket Gateway is an in-chain swap/bridge aggregator on Ethereum; the exploit drained tokens from approved users without crossing chains. source_chain == destination_chain == ethereum. The cross-chain invariant set still applies because Socket is *also* a bridge aggregator (Bungee), but the 2024-01-16 incident specifically broke the same-chain swap path.
single_chain_note.implication: Module 3 (dual-EVM fuzzer) can run with both chains pointed at the same fork; ATG will have both source and destination nodes collapse to one chain id.

root_cause_summary.vulnerable_pattern: SocketGateway.performAction calls IERC20.transferFrom(fromUser, ...) where fromUser is read from caller-supplied calldata. There is no `require(msg.sender == fromUser)` and no signed-permit alternative.
root_cause_summary.verification_bypass: Approval consent is mis-attributed: a user's prior approve(SocketGateway, X) is intended to authorize the user themselves to spend up to X via the gateway, but the gateway treats it as unconditional spending power that any caller can drive.
affected_logic: IERC20.transferFrom (downstream — unmodified, the bug is upstream caller-auth)
root_cause_summary.notes: Classified V5 (logic / business-rule bug). The pattern is the canonical 'forgot to check msg.sender' anti-pattern; what makes it expensive is the universe of pre-approved victim wallets.

exploit_characteristics.style: approval-pull drain via missing caller authorization
exploit_characteristics.notes: Attacker performed ~200 small drain transactions targeting the largest approvals. Total ~$3.3M extracted before the team paused the affected route.
